---
title: "Testing and Reconstructing the Application"
---

## Test layers

The current automated suite covers:

- RMS and peak audio measurement;
- C bridge defaults, missing models, and pinned revision;
- timeline sequencing and raw-caption preservation;
- session phase transitions;
- Gemma correction acceptance and rejection;
- science notation;
- overlay frame clamping;
- spoken command tolerance and command removal;
- memory sampling;
- WAV and transcript export;
- remote snapshot capability minimization;
- random pairing tokens;
- live server authentication, student tickets, and rate limiting;
- WebSocket protocol event extraction.

Run:

```bash
swift test
```

## Tests that require real hardware

Unit tests cannot establish:

- microphone permission UX;
- Core Audio device switching;
- projector and multi-display behavior;
- Metal model load and thermal stability;
- real classroom accuracy;
- QR scanning under glare and distance;
- institutional Wi-Fi client isolation;
- true long-session memory behavior.

These require a repeatable manual matrix.

## Recommended classroom acceptance matrix

| Area | Test |
| --- | --- |
| startup | begin speaking immediately after status becomes listening |
| distance | lecturer and students at near/middle/far positions |
| noise | ventilation, projector, typing, side conversations |
| technical text | French/English code, symbols, names, acronyms |
| latency | provisional first text and finalized correction |
| overlay | resize, move, reconnect displays, full-screen presentation |
| pause | speak while paused; verify no caption or recording appears |
| recovery | stop/start repeatedly and after model errors |
| network | iOS, Android, Windows, and network reconnection |
| questions | burst submissions, pause links, next/dismiss commands |
| archive | play WAV and compare raw/corrected JSON |
| duration | one complete lecture plus thermal cooldown |

## Voxtral benchmark

Use a fixed 16 kHz mono WAV and reference:

```bash
scripts/benchmark_voxtral.sh lecture.wav reference.txt
```

Important measures:

- model load time;
- first visible token;
- real-time factor;
- token throughput;
- word accuracy;
- provisional revision stability;
- peak and steady memory.

A real-time factor below one is necessary, but a model can still feel slow if
it waits too long before the first token.

## Reconstruction order

The safest clean-room implementation sequence is:

### 1. Domain model

Implement `CaptionSegment`, `CaptionTimeline`, and `ClassroomSession` as value
types. Test phase transitions, sequence numbers, and immutable raw text.

### 2. Manual overlay

Build the non-activating panel and feed it manual captions. Solve per-display
geometry and scrolling before adding models.

### 3. Audio capture

Capture a selected device, convert to 16 kHz mono PCM16, display a level meter,
and write a valid WAV. Test pause at this boundary.

### 4. C engine in a benchmark

Vendor the pinned runtime with license. Build a small C ABI and transcribe a
known WAV. Refuse silent CPU fallback. Record accuracy and speed.

### 5. Streaming bridge

Add model/stream opaque handles, reusable PCM conversion, bounded text copy,
serial ownership, and `AsyncStream` events.

### 6. Live captions

Start Voxtral, pre-roll silence, then open the microphone. Feed chunks, flush on
silence, and display provisional/final text.

### 7. Archive

Write streaming PCM and finalize the WAV and transcript only after model drain.

### 8. Gemma

Add the loopback process controller, serialized queue, prompt, validator, and raw
fallback. Test adversarial questions before enabling by default.

### 9. Spoken controls

Recognize fixed phrases before timeline insertion. Test cumulative provisional
strings and same-segment lecture text.

### 10. Browser sharing

Implement read-only snapshots first, then SSE, token pairing, and response
headers. Add question mutation only after a separate capability and rate policy
exist.

### 11. Packaging and documentation

Package the `Info.plist`, sign the app locally, run the complete test suite,
render this book, and conduct a real lecture soak test.

## Build and package

```bash
swift build -c release
./scripts/package_app.sh
file dist/ClassroomCaptions.app/Contents/MacOS/ClassroomCaptions
plutil -lint Resources/Info.plist
```

The packaging script creates the conventional bundle:

```text
ClassroomCaptions.app/
  Contents/
    Info.plist
    MacOS/ClassroomCaptions
    Resources/
```

It applies an ad-hoc signature. Distribution to other people should eventually
use a Developer ID signature and notarization; ad-hoc signing is appropriate
for local development, not a polished public release.

## Render documentation

HTML:

```bash
quarto render docs --to html
```

PDF through Quarto's Typst backend:

```bash
quarto render docs --to typst
```

Typst produces a PDF without requiring a separate TeX installation.

## Change checklist

Before releasing a revision:

1. inspect `git diff`;
2. run `git diff --check`;
3. run all tests;
4. compile release;
5. lint the plist;
6. render HTML and PDF;
7. launch the packaged app;
8. verify microphone and model startup;
9. scan both QR codes;
10. stop sharing and confirm old links fail;
11. test pause and final archive;
12. perform a representative live transcription.
